text: The world's largest source of post-marketing safety data helps WHO PIDM members identify and address safety issues both nationally and regionally. Pharmacists working in the dispensary at a health centre in Mondulkiri Province, Cambodia.

Signalling harm and pointing to safer use VigiBase, the World Health Organization global database of adverse event reports for medicines and vaccines, holds over 40 million reports from 180+ WHO PIDM members (as of February 2025).

This one-of-a-kind global resource supports the safer use of medicines and vaccines worldwide by helping members identify safety signals not visible from their data alone. The programme members contributing to VigiBase represent nearly 99% of the world's population. This global data set strengthens members’ ability to identify rare adverse events and assess the safety profiles of new medicines.

VigiBase contains tens of millions of adverse event reports for medicines and vaccines, accessible to all members. Its purpose is to provide evidence from which safety signals can be identified and communicated.

VigiBase uses standardised terminologies such as MedDRA and UMC’s Drug Dictionary, , to ensure structured data entry, easy data retrieval, and precise analysis, which are essential for effective and accurate assessments. As part of the automated entry process for VigiBase, each incoming report is checked against minimum report criteria for completeness.

Accuracy is ensured by using controlled vocabularies and reference classifications. This helps provide high-quality data for signal management. Analysis and interpretation of VigiBase data requires specialised knowledge and expertise.

To help users understand and analyse that data, UMC offers courses covering what kind of data VigiBase contains, how this information can be accessed, how it is protected, and how to assess and manage signals.
